blueprint_ref: Doc §2 Image 4 — Top 40 AI Terms; Doc §3 module registry row 7
coverage_notes: All 40 terms defined on-page; references optional depth only.
learn:
How to use this module

Study every term below until you can define it in your own words in under 30 seconds. References at the end are optional for deeper reading—not required to complete the module.

1 — Core AI concepts
AGI (Artificial General Intelligence)
Hypothetical systems that match or exceed human ability across the full range of cognitive tasks, not just a narrow skill. Today’s products are narrow AI; AGI remains a research and safety discussion, not a shipped product category.
AI model
A learned function (usually a large neural network) that maps inputs to outputs after training—e.g. predicting the next token, classifying text, or generating embeddings. “Model” refers to the weights + architecture, not the chat wrapper around it.
Machine learning (ML)
Algorithms that improve from data: the system adjusts parameters to reduce error on examples instead of being fully hand-coded with explicit rules for every case.
Deep learning
ML using neural networks with many layers (deep stacks of linear maps + nonlinearities). Transformers used in LLMs are a deep learning architecture.
Supervised learning
Training on labeled pairs (input, correct output). The model learns to minimize the difference between its predictions and those labels.
Unsupervised learning
Training without per-example labels—e.g. clustering, dimensionality reduction, or learning structure from raw text corpora via next-token prediction at scale.
Reinforcement learning (RL)
An agent learns by taking actions in an environment and receiving rewards or penalties. Used in games and robotics; also related to RLHF used to align LLMs with human preferences.
2 — Inference, context, and behaviour
Inference
Running a trained model on new inputs to produce outputs (forward pass). Distinct from training: no weight updates, lower cost profile, what happens in production APIs.
Context
Everything visible to the model in one forward pass: system instructions, tool results, retrieved documents, and conversation turns, usually measured in tokens.
Explainability
Techniques and practices to understand why a model produced an output—from attention visualizations to simpler surrogate models. For LLMs, perfect explainability is still limited; logging, citations (RAG), and structured outputs help in production.
Hallucination
Confident but incorrect or ungrounded content—names, citations, numbers, or APIs that were never in training or retrieval. Mitigated with RAG, tool use, evals, and human review.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Pipeline: retrieve relevant chunks from a knowledge store, inject them into the prompt, then generate. Grounds answers in your data and reduces guessing on facts.
Chain-of-thought (CoT)
Prompting the model to emit intermediate reasoning steps before a final answer. Improves performance on math, logic, and multi-step tasks when used carefully.
Context window
Maximum tokens the model can attend to in one call. Larger windows allow longer docs and history but increase cost and latency.
Cost per token
Pricing and budgeting unit for hosted LLMs: input tokens + output tokens (and sometimes cached context). Optimisation = shorter prompts, smaller models for easy tasks, batching, caching.
3 — Training, weights, and hardware
Fine-tuning
Further training a pre-trained model on a smaller domain-specific dataset to shift style, format, or behaviour—cheaper than training from scratch; often paired with evaluation and safety review.
Prompt engineering
Designing instructions, formats, and examples so the model reliably produces the shape and quality you need—still the highest-ROI lever for many products.
Tokenization
Splitting text into subword units (tokens) the model consumes. Same word can be multiple tokens; token counts drive billing and context limits.
Parameters
Learned numbers inside the network (weights and biases). “7B model” ≈ 7 billion parameters; more parameters usually mean more capacity and higher compute.
Weights
The stored parameter values after training—the actual “knowledge” in a numerical form, loaded at inference.
Embedding
A dense vector representing text (or image, etc.) in a space where similar meanings are closer. Used for search, clustering, and RAG.
Quantization
Representing weights/activations in lower precision (e.g. 8-bit, 4-bit) to shrink memory and speed inference—common for local deployment (GGUF, etc.).
Training data
The corpus used to learn the model. Quality, bias, licensing, and recency of data directly affect behaviour and compliance risk.
GPU
Parallel processor ideal for matrix math in training and inference; dominant hardware for deep learning.
TPU
Google’s ASIC family optimised for large matrix workloads; common on Google Cloud for training/inference pipelines.
Compute (AI)
Collective term for FLOPs, memory bandwidth, and wall-clock time needed to train or serve models—major driver of cost and feasibility.
Transformer
Architecture based on self-attention; parallelises across sequence positions and underpins modern LLMs. Replaced RNN/LSTM dominance for large-scale language modelling.
4 — Tools, protocols, and product surface
MCP (Model Context Protocol)
Open standard for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to an MCP host (e.g. a desktop assistant). One structured way to connect models to files, APIs, and databases.
API (in LLM products)
HTTP interface to a hosted model—chat completions, embeddings, speech, etc. Authentication, rate limits, and versioning are production concerns.
Computer vision (CV)
Models that interpret images/video: classification, detection, segmentation, OCR. Often merged with LLMs in multimodal systems.
NLP (Natural Language Processing)
Field covering understanding and generation of human language—LLMs are the dominant NLP stack today.
Chatbot
Product pattern: conversational UI over a model, often with memory, tools, and guardrails—not a distinct model type.
Generative AI
Systems that synthesise new content (text, code, images, audio) from learned patterns, as opposed to only classifying or scoring inputs.
Vibe coding
Informal term for AI-assisted development: you steer natural-language intent while the model drafts code; you still own review, tests, and security.
AI agent
System that combines a model with tools, memory, and a control loop to pursue multi-step goals—not just single-shot Q&A.
5 — Ethics, safety, and governance
AI alignment
Research and engineering to make models behave in line with human intent and values—reducing harmful outputs, deception, and misuse.
Training data quality
Accuracy, representativeness, toxicity, PII, and license posture of data. Garbage or biased data produces unreliable or unfair models.
Bias
Systematic skew in outputs that disadvantages groups or viewpoints—often from data and from optimisation targets; needs measurement and mitigation.
Privacy
Protecting personal and sensitive data in training, logging, retrieval, and tool calls (GDPR-style rights, minimisation, retention).
Regulation
Legal frameworks (e.g. EU AI Act, sector rules) governing risk classification, documentation, and deployment constraints for AI systems.
steps:
1. Read all 40 definitions without skipping. Highlight any term you cannot paraphrase.
  2. Make a two-column sheet: Term | Your one-sentence definition — fill it from memory, then self-check against this page.
  3. Pick five terms that matter most to your target role (e.g. RAG, MCP, hallucination, context window, fine-tuning) and write a short scenario where each appears in a product decision.
  4. Optional: pair each of those five with a risk (what goes wrong if the team misunderstands the term).
  5. Run the code sample to see tokenisation in action; relate token counts to “context window” and “cost per token.”
code:
# Tokenisation demo — ties to: token, context window, cost per token
# pip install tiktoken
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
text = "RAG reduces hallucinations when retrieval is good."
tokens = enc.encode(text)
print("Tokens:", len(tokens))
print("IDs (first 12):", tokens[:12])
print("Decoded round-trip:", enc.decode(tokens))
